title: UNDERSTANDING HTML WITH LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS

structured.summary: This paper investigates the applicability of off-the-shelf pretrained large language models (LLMs) to HTML understanding tasks, including semantic classification, description generation, and autonomous web navigation. By fine-tuning various LLM architectures on raw HTML snippets without HTML-specific pretraining, the work achieves state-of-the-art results with significant improvements in sample efficiency and demonstrates the superiority of encoder-decoder models like T5. It also introduces a large-scale auto-labeled description dataset and open-sources models and data to support future research.

strengths.point: Demonstrates that standard pretrained LLMs transfer effectively to HTML understanding without specialized pretraining or architectures
strengths.evidence: Fine-tuned LLMs outperform models trained from scratch, achieving up to 192× sample efficiency gains on navigation and ~12% absolute accuracy improvements on classification.

strengths.point: Comprehensive evaluation across three canonical HTML tasks covering classification, description generation, and autonomous navigation
strengths.evidence: Benchmarks include semantic classification, description generation with a new large-scale dataset, and multi-step web navigation on MiniWoB, providing a broad assessment of capabilities.

strengths.point: Clear identification of architectural preferences, with encoder-decoder T5 models outperforming encoder-only and decoder-only models
strengths.evidence: T5-based models achieve superior accuracy and success rates across tasks, highlighting the importance of bidirectional attention in HTML understanding.

strengths.point: Extensive empirical analysis including ablations on snippet extraction, action history, model size, and fine-tuning versus prompting
strengths.evidence: Ablation studies show snippet size and action history impact, and fine-tuning outperforms few-shot prompting with large LLMs.

strengths.point: Release of large auto-labeled description dataset and pretrained model weights to support reproducibility and future work
strengths.evidence: Dataset distilled from CommonCrawl with supervision from HTML label associations; open-source releases accompany the paper.

weaknesses.point: Absence of comparisons to HTML-specialized pretrained models limits assessment of novelty and rigor
weaknesses.evidence: No baselines or discussion of recent HTML-focused models like MarkupLM or WebFormer, which are state-of-the-art in HTML tasks.

weaknesses.point: Navigation evaluation restricted to synthetic MiniWoB environment limits ecological validity and real-world applicability
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments do not assess performance on real, dynamic websites with varying layouts and content.

weaknesses.point: Snippet extraction mechanism lacks thorough quantitative analysis and justification
weaknesses.evidence: Only high-level ablations are provided without detailed task-specific studies on snippet size, selection heuristics, or trade-offs.

weaknesses.point: Limited exploration of prompting methods and fine-tuning comparisons restricts conclusions about training paradigms
weaknesses.evidence: Few-shot prompting tested only once with PaLM 540B on classification; missing other prompting strategies or evaluations on multiple LLMs.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient error analysis and failure mode characterization reduce interpretability
weaknesses.evidence: No qualitative breakdowns or confusion matrices to understand common failure cases, especially in complex multi-step navigation or description generation.

weaknesses.point: Lack of detailed runtime, memory, and computational cost metrics hinders assessment of practical deployment feasibility
weaknesses.evidence: No concrete measurements of training/inference latencies or resource requirements reported.

weaknesses.point: Open-source releases lack key implementation details necessary for full reproducibility
weaknesses.evidence: Paper omits tokenizer configurations, preprocessing pipelines, training hyperparameters, and dataset splits.

weaknesses.point: No evaluation of model generalization to unseen websites, layouts, or multilingual content
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on English and do not test out-of-distribution scenarios or non-English pages.

questions.question: How do pretrained LLMs compare to specialized HTML-pretrained models on these tasks?
questions.motivation: Benchmarking against domain-specific state-of-the-art is necessary to contextualize gains and establish the novelty of applying off-the-shelf LLMs.

questions.question: How well do fine-tuned LLMs generalize to real-world, dynamic web environments beyond synthetic benchmarks?
questions.motivation: Real web pages exhibit complex, changing structures and content; understanding model robustness and scalability in such settings is critical for practical deployment.

questions.question: What are the detailed computational costs, memory requirements, and latency implications of deploying these large models for HTML tasks?
questions.motivation: Assessing feasibility for real-world applications requires understanding resource trade-offs, especially given the large model sizes involved.

questions.question: Can the snippet extraction method be further optimized or adapted dynamically to better balance informativeness and input length constraints?
questions.motivation: Improving snippet selection could enhance performance and scalability on long or complex web pages.

questions.question: How does the domain gap between LLM pretraining corpora and HTML markup affect transfer learning, and can domain-adaptive pretraining on HTML/web data improve results?
questions.motivation: Understanding and potentially mitigating domain mismatch could lead to better performance and more efficient fine-tuning.

critique_points.point_001: Absence of comparisons to HTML-specialized pretrained models limits assessment of novelty and rigor. No baselines or discussion of recent HTML-focused models like MarkupLM or WebFormer, which are state-of-the-art in HTML tasks
critique_points.point_002: Navigation evaluation restricted to synthetic MiniWoB environment limits ecological validity and real-world applicability. Experiments do not assess performance on real, dynamic websites with varying layouts and content
critique_points.point_003: Snippet extraction mechanism lacks thorough quantitative analysis and justification. Only high-level ablations are provided without detailed task-specific studies on snippet size, selection heuristics, or trade-offs
critique_points.point_004: Limited exploration of prompting methods and fine-tuning comparisons restricts conclusions about training paradigms. Few-shot prompting tested only once with PaLM 540B on classification; missing other prompting strategies or evaluations on multiple LLMs
critique_points.point_005: Insufficient error analysis and failure mode characterization reduce interpretability. No qualitative breakdowns or confusion matrices to understand common failure cases, especially in complex multi-step navigation or description generation
critique_points.point_006: Lack of detailed runtime, memory, and computational cost metrics hinders assessment of practical deployment feasibility. No concrete measurements of training/inference latencies or resource requirements reported
critique_points.point_007: Open-source releases lack key implementation details necessary for full reproducibility. Paper omits tokenizer configurations, preprocessing pipelines, training hyperparameters, and dataset splits
critique_points.point_008: No evaluation of model generalization to unseen websites, layouts, or multilingual content. Experiments focus on English and do not test out-of-distribution scenarios or non-English pages
